text: The surgical team positions robotic XI system ports for a pulmonary lobectomy, with three 8mm ports in the seventh intercostal space and two 12mm ports placed anteriorly and posteriorly. Instruments used include a bipolar dissector, cat ear forceps, and a tip-up grasper.

The surgeon views the posterior aspect of the pulmonary artery and the left lower lobe bronchus, beginning to separate the level ten L lymph nodes while maintaining dissection along the periaventusial plane. The lymph node is carefully freed up to minimize fragmentation and ensure a clean, bloodless dissection.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to perform precise dissection around the pulmonary vessels, minimizing energy scatter and ensuring safety.

The surgeon examines the subcranial space from the left side, using a glove finger technique to harvest lymph nodes suspected of metastatic involvement. The camera view is slightly obscured by a stain.
The surgeon carefully frees up the lymph node, focusing on small vessels supplying the node to minimize fragmentation. The surgical field is kept dry during this process.

The surgeon approaches the proximal hilum, targeting the proximal XL lymph node. The left main pulmonary artery is visible to the left of the carriera, while the dissection progresses proximally along the left main bronchus. The vagus nerve and aorta are visible to the right of the screen as the surgeon attempts to completely resect the lymph node due to suspected disease.

The surgeon passes the resected lymph node off using a glove and explores the surgical field to ensure complete removal. The well-developed fissure is visible, and CO₂ insufflation aids in developing the plane in the posterior hilum.
The surgeon approaches the pulmonary lobectomy by addressing the fissure last, similar to a VATS technique, to minimize air leaks in cases where the fissure is not well developed.

The surgeon harvests level XI L lymph nodes and begins exposing the pulmonary artery branches. The lingular artery branch is visible on the left side of the screen, while the posterior segment branch is also identified. The surgical field is cleared of lymph nodes to facilitate a complete lymphadenectomy and create space for stapler passage.

The surgeon uses a control stapler system with two inferior twelve millimeter trocar ports, one for assistance and one for the surgeon's left hand. This setup allows for the selection of an anterior or posterior angle for the stapler, which can carry various loads including gray, white, blue, and green.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hilar lymph nodes to expose the artery leading to the apical segment. The dissection occurs in the periaventusial plane to ensure the vessel is fully visible and to minimize interference from lymph nodes when passing the stapler.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the lingular artery, staying close to the vessel wall to free up an adjacent lymph node. This meticulous technique helps maintain a dry surgical field and contributes to a thorough lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon positions the stapler through the anterior trocar, using a caviar forcep for guidance. The twelve trocar accommodates the stapler, allowing for optimal angling. An elastic vessel loop is available for retraction, though not utilized in this instance. The surgeon practices the stapler angle before engaging the vessel.

The surgeon aligns the lung with the stapler to achieve an optimal angle without lifting or stretching the vessel excessively. After dissecting the lingular branch, the dissection proceeds from the front, between the superior and inferior pulmonary veins on the left side, creating a window underneath the parenchyma to complete the anterior aspect of the major fissure.

The surgeon uses a vessel loop to retract the lung parenchyma and positions a 45mm stapler to complete the fissure. Once the fissure is stapled, the surgeon proceeds to dissect the level XI hilar lymph nodes.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the hilar lymph nodes using the robotic system, aiming for a complete resection due to suspected N-I disease. The lymph nodes are placed in a glove and passed off through the assistant trocar.

The surgeon exposes the oropulmonary window, retracting the hilum to reveal the plane between the superior pulmonary vein and the left main pulmonary artery. Anterior level X lymph nodes are excised, freeing the superior wall of the pulmonary vein and elevating it off the first branch of the artery. The bifurcation of the branch is visible, with a lymph node obstructing the window. The surgeon decides to take the vein first, utilizing the dual twelve millimeter trocar setup for a posterior angle.

The surgeon undocks robotic arm number three and docks it onto the assistant's twelve millimeter trocar, positioning the stapler at a lower angle for a posterior approach. The stapler is closed with the anvil pointing away from the hilum to avoid collisions with the pulmonary artery. Tension is relieved to minimize bleeding from the staple line before firing the stapler using a wide load.

The surgeon uses stapler technology to assess tissue thickness and ensure proper clamping. The pulmonary artery is visible, with the vein already divided, showing the first branch and its bifurcation with the left pulmonary artery. Due to the tight window, an elastic vessel loop is fed through instead of using an instrument, enhancing visibility.

The surgeon flips the lung forward to mobilize a lymph node and increase the window for the stapler. The lung is rotated to visualize the stapler tip before advancing, ensuring correct placement. The bronchus is then taken using the stapler.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler from a posterior approach, parallel to the ongoing left lower lobe bronchus. The left upper lobe is elevated and taken at the takeoff, with all hilar nodes skeletonized and cleaned out. The surgeon then addresses the calcified AP window nodes at level five, ensuring complete removal due to nodal metastatic disease in the hilum.
